origin.question: For an axially symmetric rotating star, the time-translation Killing vector $\xi_{(t)}$ and the axial-rotation Killing vector $\xi_{(\phi)}$ satisfy the commutation relation $[\xi_{(t)}, \xi_{(\phi)}] = 0$. Consider the degenerate condition where the star's rotation vanishes and the spacetime becomes static and spherically symmetric (e.g., the exterior region of a non-rotating star). In this limit, the spacetime admits three spatial rotational Killing vectors $\xi_{(1)}, \xi_{(2)}, \xi_{(3)}$ (generating the SO(3) symmetry) in addition to $\xi_{(t)}$. Verify the limit behavior of the commutation relation: prove that in this static spherically symmetric degenerate condition, $\xi_{(t)}$ commutes with all three spatial rotational Killing vectors, i.e., $[\xi_{(t)}, \xi_{(i)}] = 0$ for $i = 1, 2, 3$. Your proof must explicitly utilize the geometric properties that define a static spacetime: the hypersurface orthogonality of $\xi_{(t)}$ (absence of cross terms $g_{ti}$ in the metric) and the time-independence of the spatial metric.
origin.solution: In a static and spherically symmetric spacetime, the metric can be locally expressed in coordinates $(t, x^j)$ (where $x^j$ are spatial coordinates such as $r, \theta, \phi$) such that it takes the block-diagonal form $ds^2 = -g_{tt}(r) dt^2 + g_{jk}(r) dx^j dx^k$. The absence of cross terms $g_{tj} = 0$ reflects the hypersurface orthogonality of the time-translation Killing vector, allowing us to globally choose $\xi_{(t)} = \partial_t$ such that it is orthogonal to the spatial hypersurfaces. The time-independence of the metric components ($\partial_t g_{ab} = 0$) guarantees that $\partial_t$ is a Killing vector. Furthermore, the spatial metric $g_{jk}(r)$ is also independent of $t$. The three spatial rotational Killing vectors $\xi_{(1)}, \xi_{(2)}, \xi_{(3)}$ that generate the SO(3) isometry group are tangent to the spatial hypersurfaces. Because the spatial geometry is spherically symmetric and time-independent, these Killing vectors can be chosen to have components that depend only on the spatial coordinates, i.e., $\xi_{(i)} = \xi_{(i)}^j(x^k) \partial_j$, with no $t$-component and $\partial_t \xi_{(i)}^j = 0$. The commutator of $\xi_{(t)}$ and any $\xi_{(i)}$ is then calculated as:$$[\xi_{(t)}, \xi_{(i)}] = [\partial_t, \xi_{(i)}^j \partial_j] = (\partial_t \xi_{(i)}^j) \partial_j + \xi_{(i)}^j [\partial_t, \partial_j] = 0 + 0 = 0.$$This verifies that in the non-rotating limit, the time-translation symmetry is preserved under all spatial rotations, meaning the full isometry group decomposes into a direct product $\mathbb{R} \times SO(3)$.
